skill.name: env-handler
skill.description: Guides safe management of environment variables by separating committed templates from local secret values.
skill.summary: Create and maintain environment variable templates while keeping real secrets in local or deployment settings.
skill.author: AayushBaniya2006

security_audit.summary: The static command execution, hardcoded URL, weak crypto, and critical combination alerts are false positives caused by markdown examples and backticked filenames. The skill is still medium risk because it instructs agents to edit environment files and coordinate secret placement, which can expose credentials if followed carelessly.

medium_findings.title: Guidance Edits Environment Files Used for Secrets
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to update .env templates and ask users to place real values in .env.local. This is legitimate configuration guidance, but it touches secret-handling workflows where mistakes can expose credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly define how .env and .env.local should be used for templates and secrets. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the guidance says real API keys should not be committed.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution indicators are backticked markdown filenames, variable names, and fenced examples. No executable script, command invocation, or user-controlled shell call is present in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown text and tables, not code that a skill runtime executes. I found no evidence of shell execution intent in SKILL.md or reference.md.

low_findings.title: Example Connection Strings and Keys Are Documentation Samples
low_findings.description: The database URL, secret key, API token, and NEXTAUTH examples document placeholder conventions. They are still sensitive concepts, but the examples instruct users not to commit real API keys.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples appear in documentation sections labeled as templates and local development examples. The surrounding security guidance says real API keys should not be committed.

low_findings.title: Network and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Non-Executable Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a localhost development value, and the secret hash string is an illustrative placeholder. I found no network request code and no cryptographic algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URL is an example configuration value and is not sent anywhere by code. The phrase secret-hash is a sample secret value, not a weak hashing implementation.

content.value_statement: Teams often mix secret values with committed configuration templates. This skill guides agents to keep templates in .env and real secrets in .env.local or deployment settings.
actual_capabilities: Defines .env as the committed template for defaults and empty secret placeholders.
Defines .env.local as the local file for real secrets and overrides.
Explains how to add sensitive variables without placing values in committed files.
Explains how to add non-sensitive public or configuration variables with defaults.
Documents server-side and client-side environment variable naming patterns.
Includes a checklist for reviewing environment variable changes.
limitations: Does not rotate secrets or verify whether existing credentials are exposed.
Does not provide automated scanning for committed secrets.
Vercel deployment guidance is brief and may need project-specific review.

use_cases.title: Add a New API Token
use_cases.description: Add an empty placeholder to .env and ask the user to place the real token in .env.local.

use_cases.title: Review Environment Templates
use_cases.description: Check that committed environment files contain defaults or empty values instead of real credentials.

use_cases.title: Prepare Deployment Configuration
use_cases.description: Map required local variables to deployment environment settings before release.

prompt_templates.title: Add One Secret
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner task for a single sensitive variable.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-handler to add a placeholder for a new sensitive variable named PAYMENT_API_KEY. Do not store the real value in committed files.

prompt_templates.title: Add Public Configuration
prompt_templates.scenario: Add a non-sensitive value with a default.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-handler to add NEXT_PUBLIC_APP_URL for local development. Set a safe default and explain where production values should live.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Template Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare template variables with local secret requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-handler to review whether each secret expected in .env.local has a matching placeholder in .env.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Deployment Variables
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced release preparation across local and hosted environments.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use env-handler to create a deployment checklist for required environment variables, including which values belong in Vercel settings.

output_examples.input: Add a new Replicate API token.
output_examples.output: Adds an empty template entry for the token, then asks the user to add the real token only in the local secrets file.

output_examples.input: Add a public app URL for local development.
output_examples.output: Adds a public configuration value with a localhost default and notes that production should use deployment settings.

output_examples.input: Review environment variable safety before commit.
output: Flags real-looking values in committed files.
Reminds the user to rotate any accidentally committed secret.

best_practices: Keep real credentials out of committed environment files.
Use empty placeholders for secrets in shared templates.
Rotate any credential that was accidentally committed.
anti_patterns: Do not create a separate .env.example when the project uses .env as the template.
Do not place production secrets in files intended for source control.
Do not expose server-only secrets with a public client-side prefix.

faq.question: Does this skill read my secret values?
faq.answer: The skill documentation tells agents where secrets should live. It does not include code that reads or uploads secret values.

faq.question: Why does it use .env instead of .env.example?
faq.answer: The skill defines .env as the committed template file for this workflow.

faq.question: Where should real API keys go?
faq.answer: Real local API keys should go in .env.local or the equivalent ignored secrets file.

faq.question: Can it configure Vercel production variables?
faq.answer: It can guide which variables to add, but users must enter production values in Vercel project settings.

faq.question: Is this safe for client-side variables?
faq.answer: It documents public client-side prefixes, but users must avoid placing server secrets in public variables.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it guides secret-handling workflows where user mistakes can expose credentials.
